you know making money by not doing any work stocks in the u.s. just in the last 10 years have had two distinct declines of north of 50 percent so to make 5 percent a year you're risking half your videos.title: The 2010 Flash Crash explained | FT Markets
videos.text: when it comes to trading us equities the solitary Trader sitting at home has never been a match for Powerhouse institutions and sophisticated computer systems such a popular perception however has been turned on its head by us authorities alleging that an independent Trader living on the outskirts of London contributed to one of the most embarrassing episodes for the equity industry and Regulators the flash crash of 2010 nearly 5 years later the long arm of the US Department of Justice has charged a Futures Trader for allegedly contributing to this flash crash VI the manipulation of S&P 500 E mini Futures what was the flash crash well in the space of 20 minutes stocks plunged and then rebounded it resulted in thousands of Trades being cancelled as some Blue Chip stocks traded at a ridiculous price of just 1 cent because Traders pulled out of the market recrimination focused on the role played by high-powered computerized Trading systems and a deeply fragmented us Equity Market where some 50 plus trading venues exist a few months later Regulators pinpointed a large sell order in equity Futures as being the trigger for the abrupt drop in prices and the evaporation of liquidity during the flash crash but any focus on S&P futures was forgotten as we saw further Tech glitches affect the US market like the Facebook IPO and the near failure of night trading in 2012 so what are e- minis this is the C's High highly popular an electronically traded Equity contract that essentially trades Around the Clock these contracts track the biggest stock market in the world the S&P 500 which has a market cap of 19 trillion it thus provides investors around the world with a fast and liquid way to trade us equities and utilize a tool that can hedge against big swings in the cash Market the explosive growth of Futures and options trading in recent years has long been seen helping maintain an orderly stock market because it quickly Narrows any sharp Divergence that we between prices and Futures and the cash Market but clearly sudden moves in Futures can reverberate with a stunning effect even across the world's largest Equity market so what can we now expect from regulators and the industry attention will focus on the role played by the equity Futures listed on the Chicago mertile Exchange and why the trading behavior of one person had such an outsized influence as alleged on the cash Equity Market we may also hear further calls for the SEC and CFT see the two separate Regulators of cash equities and Futures in the US being merged as Paul vulker suggested earlier this week and finally we should expect a deeper look at why the CME is a self-regulating exchange and whether another body should actually monitor and take action against traders who are manipulating market prices and as we have seen can have very large consequences for the broad us Equity Market

videos.title: Why the Stock Market Lost $1 Trillion for 36 Minutes
videos.text: Hey, so, have you heard of the 2010 flash crash? When a slapdash backlash trashed the market’s cash? No? Then here’s a brash rehash. So, on May 6, 2010, mostly as a result of Greece pulling some classic Greece moves, the stock market was having a pretty bad day. But then, at 2:32 pm, for seemingly no reason at all, the market started dropping like, in the words of economic expert Calvin Broadus Jr, it was hot. In the four minutes from 2:41 to 2:44 alone, it lost 300 points, and by 2:45 it had gone down a total of 600 points. But then, like Jesus after his famous three-day nap, it started to rise, and by 3:07, it had almost completely recovered. Basically, in a span of 36 minutes, $1 trillion dollars disappeared and then reappeared like it was Jeremy Piven’s hairline. So… what happened? Well, according to a controversial––we’ll get to that later––SEC report, this trillion dollar now-you-see-me now-you-don’t act can be traced to a firm called Waddell and Reed Financial selling $4.1 billion in e-mini S&P 500 futures contracts. That’s a lot of words, but basically, they’re just contracts tied directly to the overall value of the S&P 500— which is an index, used as a tool to see how the market’s doing as a whole, composed of the 500 biggest stocks on the stock market. And the good folks at Waddell and Reed Financial, or WARF, as I and nobody else calls them, decided that instead of doing the trade by having some poor intern click a button for half the day, they would have a sell algorithm handle it. Now normally, a trade that big would happen slowly, over like 5 hours, but apparently, WARF had taken a sort of Bob Marley approach and told their algorithm not to worry about time, or about price, for that matter––so it just started selling the contracts as fast as possible at whatever price was out there. As you may remember from the economics lecture your dad gave you when he refused to invest in your lemonade stand, supply and demand means that when a ton of contracts get sold, especially very quickly, prices tend to dip. “But Sam” you scream at the top of your lungs, “how did a $4 billion trade turn into a $1 trillion loss?” Well, the problem of this dumb algorithm was made worse by, you guessed it, other dumb algorithms––in particular, ones called high-frequency trading algorithms, which are designed to make lots of trades very quickly. Often, HFTs serve as something called “market makers”––basically middlemen, who buy up an asset when it's being sold, so that when buyers show up, they can turn around and sell it to them, and pocket any price difference. Now, seemingly, most of the $4.1 billion of contracts that WARF sold were quickly bought by HFTs, but here’s the thing: HFTs don’t actually like to hold contracts––their whole thing is buying them and then immediately selling them. So what happened was, a bunch of HFTs started unloading the contracts onto… other HFTs, who then unloaded those contracts onto other HFTs, and so on and so on until the contracts were basically being passed around and around and around like a toxic, market-destroying hot potato, driving down prices and creating chaos. And that led to a new problem: a lot of the algorithms that serve as market-makers reached pre-programmed thresholds that forced them to exit the market, because prices were dropping so fast and risk had gotten too high. And when the market makers pulled out, it massively reduced liquidity, which meant the market plunged even more. Now, so far, I’ve been talking about the futures market, and I told you at the beginning that the stock market collapsed. Well, that wasn’t a mistake. I don’t mank mistakes. The futures crash bled into the stock market thanks to a group of jerks called arbitrage traders. You see, when you buy a future, you’re really buying a stock that you just get later. Arbitrage traders saw S&P futures contracts getting cheap, while the S&P stocks that make up those futures were still expensive. So, they started selling their expensive regular stocks to buy the cheap future version of the same stocks, and all that rapid stock selling basically transferred the futures crash over to the stock market. So, why did it end? Well the same reason Kim and Kanye’s marriage ended: thanks to the Chicago Mercantile Exchange’s “Stop Logic Functionality.” Basically, the CME has a mechanism where, when everything starts going nuts, they force trading to stop so that everybody can have a juice box and think about what they’ve done. After a five second stop at 2:45 pm, when trading resumed, the panic had subsided, and people realized that stocks had gone down for no good reason, which now meant that stocks were basically available at a discount, which meant everybody went and bought them, which pushed the price right back up to where it had been before. So there you go: the flash crash, explained. Unless Vox has copyrighted “explained.” Uh… the flash crash… said how it works. Exceeeept… everything I just told you is actually just one theory of what happened. It’s probably the most complete theory, and it’s the one that the SEC stands by, but… the truth is, even eleven years after the flash crash, there isn’t consensus about what caused it. The SEC report received a lot of criticism from some pretty legitimate institutions, including the Chicago Mercantile Exchange. And while published peer-reviewed academic research offered an explanation centered on something called order flow toxicity, that was later called into question by further peer-reviewed research. And there are a ton of other theories, involving decentralization, spoofing, and technical glitches. In the end, the real takeaway is that financial markets that literally undergird human society have become so complicated and insane and fragile that they can wipe out $1 trillion in value, then add it all back in 36 minutes, and nobody will ever be totally sure why. Which… isn’t a very funny conclusion, so here’s a Spot-the-Difference game. Can you spot the difference between these two SEC logos? That’s right: it was a trick question. They’re the same. Now, there is one other really weird part of the flash crash: for a short period, a bunch of blue-chip stocks, worth about $40-ish, started selling for literally one penny, while at the exact same moment, a few similar other stocks started trading for $100,000. But the thing is, we have to keep these videos pretty short or our all-knowing Lord and Savior, the YouTube algorithm, will punish us. But don’t fear: I made a whole four minute video about it that you can watch right now on Nebula. Of course, the best way to get Nebula is through the CuriosityStream/Nebula bundle deal, where for less than $15 a year, with the current sale, you also get access to CuriosityStream, the documentary streaming service with fantastic content, like the very funny yet still somehow educational series Nigel Latta Blows Stuff Up. Of course, on Nebula we also have exclusive companion videos, all our normal videos early and ad-free, and big-budget Nebula originals—and you get that all not only from HAI, but from all the other independent creators you’ll be supporting. So, go get the best deal in streaming, and enhance your HAI viewing experience, by signing up at CuriosityStream.com/HAI.

videos.title: The 2010 Flash Crash: Real Footage, Real Panic, Real Lessons
videos.text: On May 6th, [music] 2010, the market didn't just move, it broke. One moment, the S&P 500 was trading normally, and then [music] seconds later, it was like somebody turned gravity off. Bids vanished. Liquidity evaporated, and the [music] entire financial system felt like it was falling through the floor. And I was there for it. Back then, I was a full-time day trader sitting in a prop firm in Boca Raton. It was a normal Thursday. The screens were humming. Traders were talking. Nothing was really unusual. And then without warning, the room went dead silent [music] because what we were watching just didn't feel real. The S&P was dropping like a stone. Blue chips were printing pennies and the order books were completely empty. I remember leaning forward right at my screen, staring at the time and sales, thinking, "This can't be happening. Something must be wrong. And then the yelling started. The phones were ringing. Traders were just screaming across the room. The disbelief, the panic, the excitement all happening at once. The entire world's liquidity was being ripped out from under us. Tick by tick. Moments like this change you as a trader. They permanently rewire how you view risk, the markets themselves, and what's actually possible when everything [music] unravels. Most traders today have heard about the flash crash, but they've never felt it. So, in this video, I'm going to take you back [music] to that moment. I've pulled the actual raw market footage and [snorts] the real pit audio, the raw unfiltered chaos from the floor as everything collapsed in real time. What you're about to hear isn't a dramatization. It's the truth. This is what it sounded like in the heart of the market as it was actively breaking. So, take a breath. Listen closely because this is one of the most violent market events in modern history. And you're about to experience it exactly [music] the way we did on that day. So, let's roll the tape. 13 out. 13 out trading right now. All right, guys. Sorry about that. There's nothing I can do about that, guys. once again on it for you here in a big way. Paper came in, huge paper sellers coming through here, guys. We got fast market up on the boards here. Once again, we have seen [music] some big paper sellers coming in. It all started out up above around 38 even here. Now, guys, talking about 25 handles [music] of solid sellside activity here, guys. They have just teased the limit down in the S&Ps would be 1150, I'm sorry, 1053 even. So, we still have a solid 60 handles of range left. EIGHT EVEN OFFER SEVEN EVEN OFFER [music] six half trading here now. SIX EVEN TRADING five even trading here now. Once again five evens do trade here. You're going to get a new five [music] even offer. Comes in four to start trading now. Three to start trading now. Three the video now. You're going to get a low rate [music] of 1103 even guys. Two alpha for two are trading here once again. I'm off of that three now. Two alphabets [music] for HERE GUYS. ALL EVENS ARE TRADING THE FINGERPRINTS ARE TRADING NINE HALF HOUR TRADING here now. Once again we [music] are bigger trade here now. Once again we are trading 112 guys we are 64 [music] handles on the day here now guys. Nine calls trade nine even offer comes in here now guys.
